title: FBLA-PBL National Bylaws & Policies
description: The official FBLA-PBL national bylaws and organizational policies document. Contains membership requirements, officer duties, competition eligibility rules, and the FBLA Code of Ethics. Essential reading for all members and officers.
author: FBLA-PBL National
fullContent:
Future Business Leaders of America–Phi Beta Lambda (FBLA-PBL) is governed by a comprehensive set of national bylaws that define the structure, purpose, and operating procedures of the organization at every level. These bylaws establish the organization's mission: to bring business and education together in a positive working relationship through innovative leadership and career development programs. Every member, advisor, and officer is expected to understand these foundational rules before participating in any official capacity.

Membership eligibility is outlined in Article III of the bylaws. Any student enrolled in a business or business-related course at an affiliated secondary school is eligible to join FBLA. Members must pay both local chapter dues and national dues to be considered active. Active membership is required to compete in any competitive event at the district, state, or national level. Members who fail to meet academic eligibility requirements set by their state may be disqualified from competition regardless of dues status.

Officer duties are specified in Articles V and VI. National officers are elected annually at the National Leadership Conference (NLC) and serve one-year terms. They are responsible for representing the membership, advocating for business education, and serving as ambassadors of professional development. Chapter officers at the local level are bound by their state's bylaws in addition to national requirements, and must maintain good academic standing throughout their term.

Competition eligibility rules are among the most carefully read sections of the bylaws. A member may only compete in a limited number of events per conference level, and certain events restrict participation to specific grade levels or class sizes. Any violation of competition rules — including academic dishonesty, misrepresentation, or use of prohibited materials — can result in disqualification and, in severe cases, suspension from future FBLA activities.

The FBLA Code of Ethics, reproduced in full within the bylaws, outlines the professional and moral standards expected of all members. It emphasizes honesty, integrity, and respect for fellow competitors, judges, and educators. Reading and internalizing the Code of Ethics is not optional — it forms the behavioral foundation that FBLA competitions and leadership development are built upon.

title: FBLA Member Handbook 2025–2026
description: Complete member guide covering chapter operations, competitive events overview, scholarship opportunities, leadership programs, and how to get the most from your FBLA membership. Updated for the 2025–2026 school year.
author: FBLA-PBL National
fullContent:
The FBLA Member Handbook is your comprehensive guide to making the most of your membership in one of the nation's premier career and technical student organizations. Updated annually to reflect the most current competitive event guidelines, scholarship deadlines, and leadership opportunities, the 2025–2026 edition is essential reading for both new members and returning veterans looking to deepen their involvement.

Chapter operations form the backbone of the FBLA experience. The handbook walks members through how a chapter is organized, the responsibilities of each officer position, how to run an effective chapter meeting using parliamentary procedure, and how to submit required reports to state and national organizations. Chapters that submit all required reports on time and maintain active membership records are eligible for the Distinguished Chapter Award — one of the highest honors a local chapter can receive.

The competitive events overview section summarizes all active events for the 2025–2026 school year, grouped by category: business administration, business communications, computer science and information technology, economics and finance, management and marketing, and organizational leadership. Each entry includes a brief description, the number of competitors allowed per chapter, and the format (individual, team, or chapter event). Use this section as your starting point when deciding which events to enter.

Scholarship opportunities are extensive. FBLA-PBL administers millions of dollars in scholarships annually through national programs, state chapter funds, and corporate partner initiatives. The handbook lists application deadlines, eligibility requirements, and tips for writing a competitive scholarship essay. Many scholarships are open only to members who have competed at the national level, so early planning is critical.

Leadership development programs described in the handbook — including the America's Business Leaders program, the Who's Who in FBLA recognition, and the state and national officer candidacy process — provide pathways for members to develop skills that extend far beyond competition season. These programs are designed to build the professional confidence, communication skills, and business acumen that colleges and employers actively seek.

title: Colorado FBLA State Conference Program 2026
description: Official program for the 2026 Colorado FBLA State Leadership Conference. Includes full event schedule, room assignments, competitive event brackets, keynote speaker bios, and awards ceremony program. April 2–4, 2026.
author: Colorado FBLA
fullContent:
The 2026 Colorado FBLA State Leadership Conference is scheduled for April 2–4, 2026, bringing together more than 2,000 students and advisors from across the state for three days of competition, networking, leadership development, and recognition. The conference is hosted at the Colorado Convention Center in Denver, with overflow event rooms at the adjacent Hyatt Regency Denver.

The conference opens on Thursday, April 2 with chapter check-in from 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM, followed by the Opening General Session at 4:30 PM. The Opening Session features the introduction of Colorado FBLA state officers, a keynote address from a Colorado-based business executive, and the announcement of special awards including Chapter of the Year nominees. Dress code for the Opening Session is business professional.

Competitive events run Friday, April 3, beginning at 7:30 AM. Objective tests are administered in the convention center's main ballrooms, divided by event category. Performance events — including presentations for Mobile Application Development, Website Design, and Public Speaking — are held in breakout rooms on the second and third floors. Competitors should arrive at their assigned room at least fifteen minutes before their scheduled time. Specific room assignments are listed in the bracket section of this program.

Saturday, April 4 begins with the Delegate Assembly at 8:00 AM, during which state officer candidates deliver their platform speeches and the membership votes on resolutions. The Leadership Awards Luncheon begins at 11:30 AM, recognizing top chapter performers. The Closing Awards Ceremony — the highlight of the conference — begins at 2:00 PM. Top finishers in competitive events are announced, and the top three teams or individuals in each event who qualify for the National Leadership Conference are recognized on stage.

Advisors and chapter officers should review the complete event schedule in the appendix of this program. All competitive event results will be posted to the Colorado FBLA website within 72 hours of the closing ceremony. Members who qualify for NLC will receive official notification via email by April 10, 2026.

title: FBLA Competitive Events Overview 2026
description: Complete guide to all 70+ FBLA competitive events for the 2025–2026 school year. Includes event descriptions, eligibility requirements, competition formats, time limits, and what judges look for. Use this to understand any event you're considering.
author: FBLA-PBL National
fullContent:
FBLA offers more than 70 competitive events organized into six major categories, giving members the opportunity to demonstrate knowledge and skills across virtually every area of business and technology. The 2025–2026 Competitive Events Overview is the authoritative reference document for understanding what each event requires, how it is scored, and what separates winning performances from average ones.

Objective test events are available in subjects ranging from Accounting I and II to Economics, Business Law, Business Communication, Intro to Business, and Computer Applications. These events consist of a 60-question, multiple-choice test administered in a controlled environment with a strict 50-minute time limit. Preparation strategy for objective tests typically involves reviewing the official study guide, practicing with prior-year tests, and targeting the specific content domains listed in the event's topic outline.

Performance events — which include Mobile Application Development, Website Design, Public Speaking, Business Presentation, and numerous others — require competitors to submit work product in advance and then present it to a panel of judges. Performance events are scored using event-specific rubrics, and the presentation component typically accounts for 30 to 40 percent of the total score. Judges are usually working professionals in the relevant field, so presenting with confidence and professional vocabulary is critical.

Team events such as Business Ethics, Emerging Business Issues, and Parliamentary Procedure require groups of three to five members to collaborate on a solution and present it jointly. Team events reward preparation, coordination, and the ability to respond to judge questions as a cohesive unit. Strong team events require weeks of practice, role assignment, and mock judging sessions.

The overview document also details chapter events — competitions where an entire chapter submits documentation of a year-long project, such as a community service initiative, a new member recruitment campaign, or a business partnership program. Chapter events recognize collective achievement and are judged on written reports, evidence of impact, and (at NLC) a chapter presentation. Every chapter should consider entering at least one chapter event each competitive year.

title: Colorado FBLA Chapter Newsletter — Spring 2026
description: The official Colorado FBLA spring newsletter featuring chapter spotlights, state conference previews, member success stories, officer installation ceremonies, and upcoming event announcements. Read about what's happening across District 12 and beyond.
author: Colorado FBLA
fullContent:
Welcome to the Spring 2026 edition of the Colorado FBLA Chapter Newsletter. As state conference approaches, chapters across Colorado have been busy hosting fundraisers, mock competitions, leadership workshops, and community outreach events. This newsletter highlights the remarkable work of our members and prepares everyone for the exciting weeks ahead.

Chapter Spotlight this issue features Cherry Creek High School's FBLA chapter, which has grown to 87 active members this school year — making it one of the largest chapters in District 12. The chapter launched a financial literacy program at two local middle schools, teaching budgeting, saving, and basic investing concepts to over 200 eighth graders. Their community impact work is being submitted as a chapter event entry for the Colorado State Conference.

In competitive event preparation news, Colorado FBLA has partnered with local business professionals to offer mock judging sessions for performance event competitors. These sessions, held at community colleges and public libraries statewide, give students the experience of presenting to real judges before the high-stakes state conference environment. Members who attended the mock judging workshops reported significantly higher confidence levels in surveys conducted by the state office.

Officer installation ceremonies have been completed at dozens of chapters across the state. Newly installed officers are encouraged to connect with the state officer team through the Colorado FBLA officer mentor program, which pairs local chapter officers with state-level mentors for monthly check-ins and professional development support throughout the year.

Looking ahead: the state conference registration deadline is March 20, 2026. All chapter advisors must submit final competitive event registrations, hotel room assignments, and delegate counts through the state conference portal by that date. Late registrations will not be accepted. Members with questions should contact their district vice president or the Colorado FBLA state office directly.

title: FBLA Code of Ethics & Professional Standards
description: FBLA's official Code of Ethics outlining the professional and ethical standards expected of all members. Covers honesty in competition, respect for competitors, proper business conduct, and the consequences of academic dishonesty. Required reading before any competition.
author: FBLA-PBL National
fullContent:
The FBLA Code of Ethics establishes the behavioral standards that all members are expected to uphold in competitions, chapter meetings, school activities, and community interactions. The Code is not merely aspirational — it carries real consequences for violations and is taken seriously by judges, advisors, and national staff. Every member should read it in full before attending any conference.

The first principle of the Code is honesty. Members are expected to present their own original work in competitions. In performance events, all code, designs, written content, and materials must be the competitor's own creation unless otherwise specifically permitted by event guidelines. Judges are trained to identify work that does not match the competitor's apparent knowledge level during Q&A, and discrepancies may trigger a formal review. Academic dishonesty — including copying code from online repositories without acknowledgment, fabricating user research, or misrepresenting team members' contributions — can result in disqualification and a ban from future FBLA competitions.

Respect for competitors, judges, and advisors is the second major theme of the Code. FBLA competitions bring together students from hundreds of different schools, backgrounds, and skill levels. Disparaging comments, unsportsmanlike conduct, or any form of harassment directed at another competitor, judge, or volunteer are considered Code violations and may be reported to conference officials. Members are expected to accept both victory and defeat with professionalism.

Professional business conduct applies to all conference activities, not just the competition floor. This includes dress code compliance, appropriate language in all verbal and written communications, responsible use of conference facilities, and respectful treatment of hotel staff, event volunteers, and the general public during conference travel.

Consequences for Code violations range from a formal warning to disqualification from the current event to a suspension from FBLA activities for a defined period. Serious violations, including those involving fraudulent representation or harassment, may be referred to school administration. Members found in violation of the Code are given an opportunity to respond before any formal action is taken. The full adjudication process is described in the national bylaws.

title: Mobile App Dev — Competition Guidelines 2026
description: Official competition guidelines for FBLA Mobile Application Development 2026. Details project requirements, allowed platforms (iOS/Android), judging criteria, presentation format (7 min + 3 min Q&A), submission checklist, and required documentation. Essential reading for every Mobile App Dev competitor.
author: FBLA-PBL National
fullContent:
The FBLA Mobile Application Development event challenges teams of one to three members to design, build, and present a fully functional mobile application that addresses a real-world problem or business need. The 2026 guidelines introduce updated judging criteria that place greater emphasis on planning documentation and innovation, reflecting the growing importance of product thinking in the software industry.

Project requirements specify that all apps must be functional on at least one mobile platform — iOS or Android — and must be demonstrated on a physical device or approved emulator during the presentation. Web applications are not eligible for this event. The app must be the team's original work, created within the current competitive year. Third-party libraries and open-source frameworks may be used but must be disclosed in the documentation package.

The required documentation package must be submitted digitally to the event proctor before the presentation begins. It must include: a planning process narrative describing how the team identified the problem and defined requirements; an architecture diagram showing the major components of the app; a data model or database schema if applicable; a testing plan documenting at least five test cases; and a reflection on what the team would improve with more time.

The presentation format consists of a seven-minute live demonstration followed by a three-minute Q&A session with judges. During the seven minutes, teams should cover the problem their app solves, a walkthrough of key features, a brief explanation of the technical architecture, and any innovative elements they are particularly proud of. The Q&A is used by judges to assess depth of technical understanding — be prepared to explain any part of your code, database, or design decisions.

Common reasons teams lose points in this event include: submitting incomplete documentation, failing to demo all features described in their materials, being unable to answer basic technical questions in Q&A, and presenting an app that does not validate user inputs or handle edge cases. Study the rubric carefully and practice your presentation with a timer at least five times before the conference.

title: Mobile App Dev — Judging Rubric & Scoring Sheet
description: The complete scoring rubric used by judges for Mobile Application Development. 110-point scale covering: Planning Process (10), Components & Architecture (10), Innovation (10), UX Design (10), Input Validation (10), Social Media Integration (5), Data Handling (5), Documentation (10), and Presentation (40). Study this deeply.
author: FBLA-PBL National
fullContent:
The Mobile Application Development judging rubric allocates 110 total points across nine scoring dimensions. Understanding exactly what judges look for in each category is the single most effective preparation strategy available to competitors. This breakdown explains each category in detail and provides actionable guidance for maximizing your score.

Planning Process (10 points) rewards teams that demonstrate systematic problem identification and solution design before writing any code. Judges want to see evidence of user research — even informal interviews or surveys — requirements gathering, wireframes or mockups, and iteration. A full 10/10 in this category requires more than a description of what the app does; it requires evidence of how the team decided what to build and why.

Components & Architecture (10 points) evaluates whether the app is built with a clear, defensible technical structure. Judges look for separation of concerns, appropriate use of design patterns (MVVM, MVC, or similar), and evidence that the team understands why their architecture decisions were made. A well-labeled architecture diagram submitted in documentation significantly helps judges assess this criterion quickly.

Innovation (10 points) is perhaps the most subjective category, but it consistently rewards apps that solve problems in ways judges haven't seen before, use device hardware in creative ways (camera, GPS, accelerometer), or integrate data from novel sources. Copying a well-known app concept with minor modifications will rarely score above 6/10 here.

UX Design (10 points) covers the visual design, navigation clarity, and overall user experience. Judges use the app themselves during evaluation. Consistent typography, logical navigation flows, appropriate contrast ratios, and a polished visual design are expected at the top score levels. Input Validation (10 points) requires both syntactic and semantic validation on all user inputs — forms, search fields, numeric entries — with clear, helpful error messages.

Presentation (40 points) is the largest single category and encompasses clarity of explanation, professional delivery, time management, and Q&A responses. Forty percent of your total score is determined in ten minutes in front of three judges. Practice, presence, and the ability to explain technical concepts in plain language will win or lose the event at this criterion.

title: React Native Expo — Getting Started Guide
description: A comprehensive getting-started guide for building mobile apps with React Native and Expo SDK. Covers project initialization, component structure, navigation with React Navigation, state management patterns, and building for iOS and Android. Recommended for Mobile App Dev competitors using React Native.
author: Expo Team
fullContent:
React Native combined with the Expo SDK is one of the most popular choices for FBLA Mobile Application Development competitors, and for good reason: the development workflow is fast, the component library is mature, and the resulting apps run on both iOS and Android from a single codebase. This guide covers the essential concepts and tools you need to get productive quickly.

Project initialization begins with installing Node.js and the Expo CLI. Running `npx create-expo-app MyApp` scaffolds a complete project with TypeScript support, a sensible folder structure, and pre-configured scripts for starting the development server, running on simulators, and building production bundles. The Expo Go app on your physical device allows instant preview of changes by scanning a QR code — no provisioning profiles or Xcode setup required for development.

Component structure in React Native follows the same mental model as React for the web, with the critical difference that all rendering primitives are native rather than HTML. `View` replaces `div`, `Text` replaces `p` and `span`, `ScrollView` replaces scrollable containers, and `FlatList` or `SectionList` handle long lists with virtualization for performance. Styling is done via JavaScript StyleSheet objects that map closely to CSS Flexbox and accepted style properties.

Navigation is handled by the React Navigation library, which provides stack, tab, and drawer navigators. The tab navigator creates the bottom tab bar common in mobile apps, while the stack navigator manages push/pop navigation with native transitions. For FBLA competitions, a clear navigation structure — where any screen is reachable in at most two taps — is a strong signal of thoughtful UX design and will be recognized by judges.

State management at the scale of an FBLA competition app is typically handled with React's built-in useState and useReducer hooks, supplemented by the Context API for global state like authentication or user preferences. For persistent data, AsyncStorage provides a simple key-value store. For more structured data, SQLite (via expo-sqlite) or a cloud database like Firebase Firestore are excellent choices that demonstrate data handling sophistication to judges.

title: Mobile App UI/UX Design Principles
description: Best practices for mobile app user interface and user experience design. Covers accessibility standards, touch target sizing, color contrast ratios, typography scales, navigation patterns, and how to design for both iOS and Android. Judges score heavily on UX quality — read this guide.
author: FBLA-PBL National
fullContent:
User experience design is no longer a specialty discipline reserved for large product teams — it is an expected competency for every mobile developer, and FBLA judges have consistently increased the weight they place on UX quality in their scoring. This guide covers the principles that separate apps that feel professional from those that feel like student projects.

Touch target sizing is the most commonly violated accessibility principle in student-built mobile apps. Every interactive element — buttons, links, icons, list items — must have a minimum touch target of 44x44 points as specified by both Apple's Human Interface Guidelines and Google's Material Design. Elements smaller than this are consistently frustrating to use, especially for users with larger fingers or motor impairments. Judges who interact with your app during evaluation will notice undersized targets immediately.

Color contrast ratios must meet WCAG 2.1 Level AA requirements: a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text (18pt or 14pt bold). Free online tools such as the WebAIM Contrast Checker make it easy to verify that your chosen color palette meets these standards. Using brand colors that fail contrast requirements is a common mistake that costs points in the UX and accessibility dimensions.

Typography scales create visual hierarchy that guides users through your interface. A disciplined type scale — for example, 22pt for screen titles, 17pt for section headers, 15pt for body text, 13pt for captions and labels — makes your app feel intentional and well-designed. Consistency matters: if body text is 15pt on one screen and 13pt on another for no apparent reason, it signals a lack of design polish.

Navigation patterns should follow platform conventions. iOS users expect tab bars at the bottom and navigation bars at the top with a back button. Android users expect the same tab bar pattern but are also accustomed to gesture-based back navigation. Building your app with platform-native navigation patterns reduces the cognitive load on judges and real users alike, allowing them to focus on your app's unique features rather than figuring out how to move between screens.

title: Winning Presentation Tips — Mobile App Dev
description: Tips and strategies from past FBLA Mobile App Dev champions on how to ace the 7-minute presentation. Covers how to demo your app effectively, what questions judges typically ask, how to explain your architecture without losing non-technical judges, and presentation flow techniques.
author: Colorado FBLA
fullContent:
The seven-minute presentation is where state and national championships are won and lost. Past champions consistently cite the same set of habits and strategies that elevated their performance above technically comparable competitors. This guide distills those lessons into actionable advice you can implement in your next practice session.

Open with the problem, not the solution. Many teams make the mistake of starting their presentation by launching directly into app features. Judges who don't understand the problem your app solves won't appreciate the elegance of your solution. Spend the first sixty seconds framing the real-world problem with a brief story or statistic that makes the need for your app feel concrete and urgent. Then introduce your app as the answer.

Demo live features that work. It sounds obvious, but judges report that a surprising number of teams demo features that are incomplete, crash under certain inputs, or require workarounds. Before conference day, test every feature you plan to demo on the exact device you will use, with the exact data you will show. Have a backup plan — a screen recording on your phone — in case the device misbehaves. Never apologize to judges for features that don't work; it undermines confidence in everything else you've built.

Explain your architecture in one sentence before diving into the diagram. Non-technical judges make up a portion of every panel, and they feel lost when competitors jump straight into acronyms and technical vocabulary. A one-sentence summary — "Our app uses a Firebase backend for real-time data sync and follows the MVVM pattern to keep our business logic separate from the UI" — gives everyone a frame of reference before the technical details begin.

Prepare for the top ten judge questions. Based on feedback from previous years, the most common Q&A questions include: How does the app handle no internet connection? What would you add with more time? How do you handle user data privacy? What happens when a user enters invalid input? Why did you choose this platform over the alternatives? Prepare clear, confident answers to each of these and your Q&A score will reflect it.

title: MVVM Architecture Pattern for Mobile Apps
description: Explanation of the Model-View-ViewModel (MVVM) architectural pattern commonly used in mobile app development. Explains the separation of concerns between data models, view models (business logic), and views (UI). Includes code examples in React Native and Swift. Directly relevant to the architecture judging criterion.
author: FBLA Technical Resources
fullContent:
The Model-View-ViewModel (MVVM) pattern is one of the most widely used architectural approaches in professional mobile app development, and it is explicitly evaluated by FBLA judges under the Components & Architecture scoring criterion. Understanding and correctly implementing MVVM is a reliable way to earn full marks in that category and demonstrate engineering maturity to judge panels.

The Model layer represents the data structures and business rules of your application. In a React Native app, this includes your TypeScript interfaces or JavaScript objects that define the shape of your data, the functions that validate and transform that data, and any data access logic (API calls, database queries) that retrieves or persists information. The Model has no knowledge of how data will be displayed — it exists purely to represent and manage application data.

The ViewModel layer acts as the intermediary between the Model and the View. It exposes data in a format that the View can consume directly, performs filtering, sorting, and other derived computations, and handles user action logic that results in Model updates. In React Native, ViewModels are typically implemented as custom hooks — for example, `useResourcesViewModel` — that manage local state, call data access functions, and return only the values and functions that the View needs.

The View layer consists of your React Native components — the actual JSX that renders UI elements on screen. A well-structured View in MVVM contains minimal logic: it reads data from the ViewModel, renders it, and calls ViewModel functions in response to user interactions. If you find yourself writing complex conditional logic, data transformations, or API calls directly inside a component's render function, that is a signal to move that code into the ViewModel.

The practical benefits of MVVM in a competition context are significant. Separation of concerns makes your code easier to explain to judges, easier to test (the ViewModel can be unit tested without rendering UI), and easier to maintain during the iterative development process. When a judge asks "how is your business logic organized?", competitors who can point to a clear ViewModel layer give a much more impressive answer than those who have all logic embedded in components.

title: Input Validation Best Practices
description: Guide to implementing both syntactic and semantic input validation in mobile applications. Covers syntactic validation (format checking, regex patterns, length limits) and semantic validation (business logic checks, uniqueness, consistency). Includes code examples and explains why validation quality is heavily scored in FBLA.
author: FBLA Technical Resources
fullContent:
Input validation is worth ten full points on the FBLA Mobile App Dev rubric, yet it is one of the most commonly under-implemented areas in student submissions. Many apps validate that a field is not empty and call it done — but real-world professional standards (and FBLA judges) expect both syntactic and semantic validation on every user input.

Syntactic validation checks that input conforms to the expected format before any business logic is applied. Examples include: verifying that an email address matches the pattern user@domain.tld, ensuring a phone number contains exactly 10 digits, checking that a username is between 3 and 20 characters and contains only allowed characters, and confirming that a numeric input falls within an expected range (e.g., age between 1 and 120). Regular expressions are commonly used for syntactic validation, but React Native also has native input props like `keyboardType` and `maxLength` that enforce basic constraints at the input level.

Semantic validation goes a step further by checking whether the input makes sense in the context of the application's data and business rules. Examples include: verifying that a username is not already taken before allowing account creation, checking that a start date is before an end date when both are provided, confirming that a credit amount does not exceed an account's available balance, and validating that two password fields match before accepting a new password. Semantic validation typically requires a round-trip to your database or API and may involve asynchronous operations.

Error messages are as important as the validation logic itself. A message that says "Invalid input" is nearly useless to a user who doesn't understand what was wrong. Best practice is to display specific, actionable messages: "Email must be in the format name@example.com", "Username must be at least 3 characters", "Start date cannot be after end date". Position error messages directly below the relevant input field, display them in a color with sufficient contrast (red or amber on white background), and clear them when the user begins correcting the field.

For an FBLA demonstration, plan a specific validation demo sequence. Show a form with intentionally invalid input, submit it, point out the error messages that appear, correct the input, and show successful submission. Judges who see this sequence understand immediately that you have implemented real validation rather than simply checking for empty fields. This focused demo can be the difference between 6/10 and 10/10 on the validation criterion.

title: 2025 Mobile App Dev Winner — Case Study
description: A detailed case study of the 2025 FBLA National Champion in Mobile Application Development. Analyzes their app architecture, key features, presentation strategy, and the specific rubric points they maximized. Includes judge feedback and what made their app stand out from hundreds of competitors.
author: FBLA-PBL National
fullContent:
The 2025 FBLA National Champion in Mobile Application Development built a career networking app specifically designed for high school students seeking their first internship or part-time job. The app connected students with local employers, allowed students to build a digital portfolio, and provided AI-powered resume feedback. What set this team apart was not the complexity of the technology — it was the quality of their planning documentation and the professionalism of their presentation.

From a technical architecture standpoint, the winning team used React Native with Expo for the frontend, Firebase Firestore for real-time data storage, Firebase Authentication for secure login, and the OpenAI API for resume feedback features. The team's architecture diagram clearly showed the data flow between components, and they could articulate why they made each technology choice during Q&A. Judge feedback specifically praised the team's understanding of their own codebase — every judge question was answered confidently without hedging.

The planning documentation submitted by the winning team was notably thorough. It included a five-page planning narrative that described three rounds of user research (initial interviews, prototype testing, and post-build feedback sessions), a complete set of wireframes for every screen, a data model with entity-relationship diagrams, and a testing log showing forty individual test cases with pass/fail results. This documentation package received the maximum planning score — something only a handful of teams achieve at the national level.

The presentation itself was tight and purposeful. The team opened with a thirty-second story about a student who struggled to find internships because she had no way to showcase her skills to local employers. They then demonstrated four core features in sequence, each tied directly back to a rubric criterion. They ended with a brief technical summary that satisfied technical judges without losing the non-technical panel members. The Q&A responses were concise — no rambling — and the team maintained strong eye contact throughout.

The key lessons from this case study: solve a real problem with clear evidence that it exists; build thorough documentation from day one rather than writing it the week before the conference; practice your presentation until every sentence is deliberate; and never apologize to judges for anything. The winning team's app had minor visual imperfections — but their confidence and preparation made those irrelevant.

title: Website Design — Competition Guidelines 2026
description: Official guidelines for FBLA Website Design 2026. Covers website requirements (must be functional, not a mockup), allowed technologies, hosting requirements, accessibility standards (WCAG 2.1), judging criteria, and presentation format. Read carefully — judges check for accessibility compliance.
author: FBLA-PBL National
fullContent:
The FBLA Website Design event challenges teams of one to three students to design and build a fully functional website addressing a specific business scenario released by FBLA-PBL at the start of the competitive year. The 2026 guidelines specify that the website must be live and accessible via public URL at the time of judging — static mockups or locally hosted sites are not permitted.

Allowed technologies for 2026 include any standard web technology stack: HTML5, CSS3, JavaScript, and popular front-end frameworks such as React, Vue, or Angular are all permitted. Server-side technologies including Node.js, Python (Flask/Django), and PHP are allowed for dynamic functionality. Teams may use CSS frameworks such as Bootstrap or Tailwind CSS but must be able to explain every styling decision to judges. AI-generated code must be disclosed in the documentation package and the team must demonstrate understanding of how it works.

Hosting requirements are straightforward: the site must be accessible on a standard web browser without requiring any plugins, special software, or account creation by the judge. All features must work on both desktop and mobile viewport sizes. Judges access the site on a laptop during judging and may also test it on a mobile device. Sites that are not mobile-responsive will lose significant points in the Design & Visual Appeal and Functionality & Navigation categories.

Accessibility standards for 2026 require compliance with WCAG 2.1 Level AA at a minimum. This means all images must have descriptive alt text, color is never the sole means of conveying information, all functionality must be keyboard accessible, and color contrast ratios must meet the 4.5:1 minimum for normal text. Judges use automated accessibility checking tools (such as WAVE or Lighthouse) as part of their evaluation process — sites with accessibility failures will lose points in the Accessibility & Standards Compliance category.

The presentation format is five minutes for demonstration followed by three minutes of Q&A. Teams should plan their demo to cover: the business scenario they were addressing, the major pages and features, a brief technical overview, and any innovative design decisions they are proud of. Practice navigating the live site smoothly — clicking errors, broken links, or slow page loads are highly visible to judges during a live demo.

title: Website Design — Judging Criteria & Rubric
description: Complete scoring rubric for FBLA Website Design. Covers: Design & Visual Appeal (25), Functionality & Navigation (25), Content Quality (20), Accessibility & Standards Compliance (15), Presentation (15). Total 100 points. Understanding this rubric helps you prioritize what to build.
author: FBLA-PBL National
fullContent:
The FBLA Website Design scoring rubric allocates 100 points across five categories. Each category reflects a core dimension of professional web development, and understanding how judges assign points within each category is essential for building a site that competes at the highest level.

Design & Visual Appeal (25 points) evaluates the overall aesthetic quality of the site. Judges look for a cohesive color palette, consistent typography, appropriate use of white space, high-quality imagery, and a visual hierarchy that guides users' attention to the most important content. Maximum scores in this category go to sites that look indistinguishable from professionally designed commercial websites. Common ways teams lose points here: inconsistent fonts across pages, low-resolution images, excessive use of decorative elements that clutter the layout, and color choices that don't reflect the business scenario's brand.

Functionality & Navigation (25 points) assesses whether the site works correctly and is easy to navigate. Judges click every link, submit every form, and test every interactive element. Broken links, forms that don't submit correctly, JavaScript errors in the console, and navigation that doesn't work on mobile all directly reduce this score. The site must be logically organized — a user should be able to find any piece of information within two clicks from the homepage.

Content Quality (20 points) covers whether the textual and visual content on the site is appropriate, well-written, and relevant to the business scenario. This includes the accuracy of business information, professional writing quality, absence of spelling and grammar errors, and whether the content effectively serves the target audience described in the scenario. Generic placeholder content such as "Lorem ipsum" text will result in near-zero points in this category.

Accessibility & Standards Compliance (15 points) is evaluated using a combination of automated tools and manual inspection. Teams should run their site through Google Lighthouse and achieve an accessibility score of 90+ before the conference. Additionally, judges may manually check for alt text on images, heading hierarchy, keyboard focus indicators, and form label associations.

Presentation (15 points) in Website Design is a smaller portion of the total than in Mobile App Dev, but it is still significant. A polished, confident five-minute demonstration that clearly communicates the site's purpose and technical implementation will reliably earn 13–15 points in this category.

title: Web Accessibility Guide — WCAG 2.1
description: Guide to Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1 for FBLA Website Design competitors. Explains AA and AAA compliance levels, color contrast requirements, keyboard navigation, alt text for images, and screen reader compatibility. Accessibility is a scored criterion — don't ignore it.
author: W3C
fullContent:
The Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1, published by the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C), define the international standard for accessible web design. For FBLA Website Design competitors, WCAG 2.1 Level AA compliance is required by the event guidelines and is directly evaluated in the Accessibility & Standards Compliance scoring criterion. Understanding the standard is not optional — it is a competition requirement.

WCAG 2.1 is organized around four principles, summarized by the acronym POUR: Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, and Robust. Perceivable means all non-text content (images, icons, charts) must have text alternatives, and media must have captions or transcripts. Operable means all functionality must be achievable using a keyboard alone — no functionality should require a mouse. Understandable means text must be readable and predictable, and forms must provide helpful error messages. Robust means the site must work with current and future assistive technologies, including screen readers.

Color contrast is the most commonly checked accessibility criterion in competition. Level AA requires a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1 between text and its background for normal-sized text, and 3:1 for large text (18pt regular or 14pt bold). Free tools like the WebAIM Contrast Checker, the Colour Contrast Analyser desktop app, and Chrome's built-in DevTools color picker all provide instant contrast ratio calculations. Before finalizing your color palette, verify every text-background combination.

Keyboard navigation compliance means every interactive element — links, buttons, form fields, dropdown menus, modals — must be reachable and activatable using only the Tab key and Enter/Space. Focus indicators (the visible outline that shows which element is currently focused) must never be removed via CSS (`outline: none` is a common accessibility violation). Custom components built with div elements instead of semantic HTML may need explicit ARIA roles and keyboard event handlers to be accessible.

Images must have alt text that describes their content or function. Decorative images — those used purely for visual effect — should have empty alt text (`alt=""`) so screen readers skip them. Informative images — charts, diagrams, photos that convey content — must have descriptive alt text that conveys the same information as the image. Judges using screen readers or automated tools will identify missing or inadequate alt text immediately.

title: HTML5 & CSS3 Best Practices for Competition
description: Best practices for writing clean, standards-compliant HTML5 and CSS3 in FBLA Website Design competitions. Covers semantic HTML, CSS Grid and Flexbox layouts, responsive design, performance optimization, and code organization. Judges review source code — make it clean.
author: FBLA Technical Resources
fullContent:
FBLA Website Design judges include working web developers who review source code as part of their evaluation. A site that looks polished on the surface but has disorganized, non-semantic, or invalid HTML underneath will lose points in the Standards Compliance category. This guide covers the code quality standards expected at the competition level.

Semantic HTML means using the correct HTML elements for their intended purpose, not just any element that achieves the desired visual result. Navigation menus should use `<nav>` elements. Page sections should use `<section>` or `<article>` rather than generic `<div>` containers. Form labels must be associated with their inputs via the `for`/`id` relationship. Page-level headings should use `<h1>` through `<h6>` in a logical hierarchy — never skip heading levels for visual effect, and never use heading elements to achieve a specific font size when CSS should be used instead. Semantic HTML improves accessibility, SEO, and code readability simultaneously.

CSS organization follows several accepted patterns at the professional level. CSS custom properties (variables) for your color palette, spacing scale, and typography scale make global design changes trivial and demonstrate an understanding of design systems. BEM (Block Element Modifier) naming convention for class names creates a clear, predictable relationship between HTML structure and CSS selectors. Separating layout (Grid/Flexbox) from visual styling (colors, typography) in your CSS files makes both easier to read and modify.

CSS Grid is the preferred tool for two-dimensional page layouts — defining rows and columns, placing items within a grid, and creating complex layouts that adapt responsively. Flexbox excels at one-dimensional layouts: aligning items within a row or column, distributing space between elements, and centering content. Using both tools appropriately and being able to explain the difference to judges demonstrates genuine CSS proficiency.

Performance optimization should include compressing all images before uploading (JPEG for photos, PNG for graphics with transparency, WebP for modern browsers), minifying CSS and JavaScript for production builds, and avoiding large unused CSS frameworks. Google Lighthouse measures performance alongside accessibility — aiming for a Lighthouse score of 90+ across Performance, Accessibility, Best Practices, and SEO is a strong competition target. Run Lighthouse in Chrome DevTools before the conference and address any flagged issues.

title: Website Design Inspiration — Past Winners
description: Gallery of past FBLA Website Design winning sites with analysis of what made each one successful. Covers design trends, feature implementations, and how winners approached the annual topic. Excellent for benchmarking your own design against competition-winning standards.
author: FBLA-PBL National
fullContent:
Studying past winners is one of the most efficient preparation strategies available to FBLA Website Design competitors. Winners have already been evaluated by the same judges using the same rubric you will face, and their sites represent the clearest benchmark available for what a top-scoring submission looks like. This gallery covers national and state-level winners from the past three years.

The 2025 National Champion site was built for a sustainable fashion e-commerce company. The team used a clean, minimal design aesthetic with a forest green and off-white color palette that reinforced the brand's environmental focus. Every product page included detailed sustainability metrics, and the checkout flow featured a carbon offset calculator — an innovative feature that earned them top marks in Design & Visual Appeal and impressed judges enough to fuel a perfect presentation score. The site scored 96/100, with the only deductions coming from minor keyboard navigation issues on the product filter component.

The 2024 National Champion built a site for a community health clinic. What distinguished their entry was the exceptional content quality — all medical information on the site was accurate, written at an appropriate reading level for the target audience, and organized with a clear information architecture. The team invested heavily in content strategy before beginning visual design, and it showed. Judges commented that the site felt genuinely useful rather than designed purely to score rubric points.

Common design trends among recent winners include: large, high-quality hero images with clear value propositions; card-based content layouts that work equally well on desktop and mobile; micro-interactions (subtle hover effects, smooth transitions) that add polish without distracting from content; and clear calls-to-action that guide users toward the site's primary goal. These trends reflect professional web design standards and signal to judges that competitors are aware of current industry practices.

The most common reason sites fail to place in the top tier is not technical inadequacy — it is misalignment between the site's design and the business scenario. Teams that build visually impressive sites but ignore the specific requirements of the scenario (target audience, business goals, required pages) consistently lose to teams with simpler designs that perfectly address what was asked. Read the scenario carefully, identify the core business goals, and design every page with those goals in mind.

title: Responsive Design with CSS Grid & Flexbox
description: Practical guide to building responsive websites using modern CSS layout techniques. Explains CSS Grid for two-dimensional layouts, Flexbox for one-dimensional layouts, and how to combine them effectively. Includes media query strategies for mobile, tablet, and desktop breakpoints.
author: FBLA Technical Resources
fullContent:
Responsive design is no longer an advanced feature — it is a baseline expectation for every website entered in FBLA Website Design competition. Judges explicitly test mobile viewport behavior, and sites that break below 768px viewport width will lose significant points in both Functionality & Navigation and Design & Visual Appeal. This guide covers the two modern CSS layout tools that make responsive design systematic and manageable.

Mobile-first design methodology means writing your base CSS for small screens first, then using `min-width` media queries to layer in desktop-specific styles. This approach tends to produce cleaner code than the alternative (designing for desktop and overriding styles for mobile) because mobile layouts are inherently simpler — there is less to undo. The standard breakpoints used in most professional projects are 480px (small phones), 768px (tablets and large phones), 1024px (small laptops), and 1280px (standard desktop). Using CSS custom properties for breakpoint values makes them easy to manage consistently across a stylesheet.

CSS Grid is the right tool for page-level layout. A typical website layout — header spanning full width, a three-column content grid, a full-width footer — maps directly to Grid's row and column structure. The `grid-template-areas` property allows you to define named layout zones and reassign them at different breakpoints with minimal code. For example, a three-column layout on desktop can collapse to a single column on mobile with a single `grid-template-columns` override inside a media query.

Flexbox is the right tool for component-level layout — the items within a nav bar, a card component's internal structure, a row of buttons. `display: flex` with `flex-wrap: wrap` is the most common pattern for creating rows of items that automatically reflow to multiple rows when space is constrained. `justify-content` and `align-items` control alignment along the main and cross axes, giving you precise control over spacing and positioning without the hacks required in older CSS.

Combining Grid and Flexbox in the same project is not a contradiction — it is best practice. A common pattern is to use Grid for the outermost page structure and Flexbox for the components within each Grid area. Testing your responsive layout at every breakpoint before the conference is essential: resize the browser window slowly from desktop width down to 320px and fix any point where the layout breaks, overflows, or becomes unusable. Mobile judges will notice immediately.
